coredata.dc:title: Repurposing expired metformin to fluorescent carbon quantum dots for ratiometric and color tonality visual detection of tetracycline with greenness evaluation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Mohammed Ameen, Sameera Sh.
Bedair, Alaa
Hamed, Mahmoud
Mansour, Fotouh R.
Omer, Khalid M.
coredata.dc:description: Repurposing expired drugs is highly significant as it prevents the accumulation of pharmaceutical wastes, optimizes resource usage, and reduces environmental harm. Metformin, used for type II diabetes, lowers blood sugar via decreasing liver glucose production and increasing muscle insulin sensitivity. However, the growing number of expired tablets annually necessitates a strategic approach for repurposing or reusing these tablets. Herein, we develop a simple hydrothermal approach for converting expired metformin to valuable fluorescent carbon quantum dots (m-CQDs) with sizes smaller than 10 nm. The m-CQDs displayed a blue emission at 420 nm when excited at 350 nm. Interestingly, when tetracycline (TC) was added, the blue emission diminished, and a greenish-yellow emission emerged at 530 nm. This emission tunning was the resulting of combination of inner filter effect and aggregation induced emission. Leveraging this dynamic emission tunning, we developed both ratiometric-based fluorescence and color tonality-based visual detection methods for detecting TC in pharmaceuticals and wastewater. More importantly, three assessment tools, ComplexMoGAPI, AGREE, and BAGI were used to evaluate the environmental sustainability of the proposed analytical method. The evaluation confirmed the sustainability of the method and the alignment with the greenness guidelines for analytical methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Tetracycline
Visual detection
Color tonality
Waste management
Greenness evaluation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A-B) TEM of m-CQDs. C) Histogram of particle size diameter of m-CQDs. D) XRD m-CQDs. E) FTIR m-CQDs.
(A) XPS survey of m-CQD. (B) C 1s of m-CQD. (C) O 1s of m-CQD. (D) N 1s of m-CQD. (E) Fluorescence spectrum of m-CQD.
(A) Absorption spectra of TC, m-CQD, m-CQD @TC, and the excitation spectrum of m-CQD (black line). (B) Fluorescence spectra of TC, m-CQD, and m-CQD @TC.

(A) Emission spectra of the m-CQDs via the addition of different concentrations of TC. (B) calibration graph of the emission intensity of m-CQDs vis TC concentrations. (C) smartphone taken pictures of the fluorescence emission of m-CQDs with various concentration of TC. (D) The calibration graph of the (G+R)/B Value of the m-CQDs versus TC concentrations.

Greenness and Applicability assessment of the proposed method using (A) ComplexMoGAPI [55] (B) Analytical Greenness calculator (AGREE) [56] (C) Blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI) [61]. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic illustration for synthesis of m-CQDs and sensing towards of TC.
Comparison of several reported luminescent-based probes for sensing of TC.
Sensing of TC in real samples.

head.ce:title: Repurposing expired metformin to fluorescent carbon quantum dots for ratiometric and color tonality visual detection of tetracycline with greenness evaluation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Qliasan St. 46002, Sulaymaniyah, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Sulaimani, Qliasan St. 46002, Sulaymaniyah, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Repurposing expired drugs is highly significant as it prevents the accumulation of pharmaceutical wastes, optimizes resource usage, and reduces environmental harm. Metformin, used for type II diabetes, lowers blood sugar via decreasing liver glucose production and increasing muscle insulin sensitivity. However, the growing number of expired tablets annually necessitates a strategic approach for repurposing or reusing these tablets. Herein, we develop a simple hydrothermal approach for converting expired metformin to valuable fluorescent carbon quantum dots (m-CQDs) with sizes smaller than 10 nm. The m-CQDs displayed a blue emission at 420 nm when excited at 350 nm. Interestingly, when tetracycline (TC) was added, the blue emission diminished, and a greenish-yellow emission emerged at 530 nm. This emission tunning was the resulting of combination of inner filter effect and aggregation induced emission. Leveraging this dynamic emission tunning, we developed both ratiometric-based fluorescence and color tonality-based visual detection methods for detecting TC in pharmaceuticals and wastewater. More importantly, three assessment tools, ComplexMoGAPI, AGREE, and BAGI were used to evaluate the environmental sustainability of the proposed analytical method. The evaluation confirmed the sustainability of the method and the alignment with the greenness guidelines for analytical methods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Repurposing expired drugs into useful materials presents a compelling solution from an environmental standpoint, offering multiple benefits [1,2]. By repurposing these drugs, waste generation is reduced, thereby minimizing the environmental burden associated with their disposal. Moreover, repurposing helps mitigate chemical pollution risks by securely containing and utilizing the drugs’ components in beneficial applications, thereby preventing their release into the environment [3]. Adopting these approaches is consistent with the key concepts of a circular economy, which promotes the production of eco-friendly products and advances environmental responsibility across diverse sectors and communities. This, in turn, plays an important role in guiding us towards a more environmentally sustainable future [4]. Hence, our focus lies in repurposing expired drugs into valuable precursors, which can ultimately be used for an application.

Metformin, predominantly prescribed to manage type 2 diabetes, has witnessed a remarkable surge in annual prescriptions over recent years. Currently, metformin is the most widely prescribed oral antidiabetic drug globally, accounting for 45–50 % of all prescriptions and being used by more than 150 million individuals annually [5]. This substantial growth underscores the drug’s pivotal role as a primary treatment for diabetes. Its efficacy in reducing blood sugar levels, along with its favorable safety profile and cost-effectiveness, cements metformin as a preferred choice for diabetes management. As diabetes prevalence continues to escalate globally, metformin’s significance in modern healthcare remains paramount. Therefore, repurposing of the expired metformin is highly essential step from the environmental and economic perspectives.

Detection of antibiotics, especially tetracycline, is highly crucial across healthcare, food safety, and environmental sectors [6–8]. Tetracyclines are widely used in medicine and agriculture, emphasizing the need for accurate detection to prevent antibiotic resistance [9]. In healthcare, detection aids in patient treatment and monitoring, while in food safety, it ensures compliance with regulations and protects consumers. Environmental monitoring assesses antibiotic pollution impacts [10]. Reliable detection is vital for responsible antibiotic use, safeguarding public health, and preserving the environment. For this purpose, many methods have been designed for detection of antibiotics, such as electrochemical, colorimetric, fluorescence, and electrogenerated chemiluminescence methods. The above methods have used many materials, such as organic fluorophores [11], metal and metal oxide nanoparticles [12,13], metal–organic frameworks [14–16], and carbon dots.

Carbon dots or carbon nanodots (CDs), small carbon nanoparticles under 10 nm in size, exhibit unique properties and versatile applications [17,18]. Synthesized through methods like hydrothermal treatment, microwave-assisted synthesis, and electrochemical approaches, they offer scalability, stability and eco-friendliness. With strong photoluminescence, biocompatibility, and tunable electronic properties, CDs find wide-spread applications in biomedicine, optoelectronics, biochemical sensing, and catalysis. In bioanalysis, they were used for rapid and selective detection of analytes, such as detection of pharmaceuticals [19,20], metal ions [21–23], various biomarkers [24,25]. Thus, CDs hold promise for driving technological innovation across disciplines, addressing societal needs, and paving the way for sustainable solutions. One starting materials for preparation of carbon dots are organic or carbon-based materials. From here, pharmaceutical wastes are one of the best sources for this purpose. Hence one can serve circular economy via reduction and reuse of the waste.

Ratiometric-based fluorescence is a crucial mode for analysis because it enhances measurement accuracy by using the ratio of two emission wavelengths, reducing the impact of external variables. This method ensures more reliable results, making it particularly useful in sensitive applications like biosensing and imaging. Moreover, it enables the detection of subtle changes in color or hue (color tonality), which is crucial for accurate diagnostics in resource-limited settings [26–32].

In the present work, metformin wastes are repurposed for the preparation of blue fluorescent carbon quantum dots (m-CQDs) using eco-friendly hydrothermal procedure, and water as a lone solvent. Then, m-CQDs will be used for the detection of tetracycline (TC), as addition of TC-induced quenching of the blue fluorescence and evolved greenish-yellow fluorescence at 530 nm. Emission color tuning by TC resulted in designing ratiometric-based fluorescence and color tonality visual detection mode for TC. Finally, evaluation of the greenness of the whole method is performed using common assessment protocols. Scheme 1 summarizes the main steps for preparation and application of m-CQDs.

The chemicals and solvents used throughout this work were of analytical grades and used without further purification. Tetracycline (TC), and all other metal ions were of nitrate and chloride source obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Sigma Aldrich, Germany). All the following chemicals were procured from Alfa Aeser Germany: silver nitrate (AgNO3), citric acid (CA), NaOH, methanol, various carbohydrates such as glucose (Glu), lactose (Lac), fructose (Fru), and sucrose (Suc), amino acids like histidine (His), tyrosine (Tyr), tryptophan (Trp), lysine (Lys), glycine (Gly), asparagine (Asp), glutathione (Glut), phenylalanine (Phe), serine (Ser), as well as uric acid (UA), NaCl, KCl, Al(NO3)3, CoCl2, FeCl3, Cr(NO3)3, CdCl2, MnCl2, and BaCl2. A dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 500 Daltons was obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Pure deionized water was used to prepare all the solutions (18.2 MΩ cm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: All information about instrumentations and synthesis sections are briefly displayed in the Supporting Information
Fluorescence platform for sensing of TC
ce:section.ce:para: All the fluorescence studies were taken at RT (25 ± 2 °C), using 350 nm as excitation working wavelength. To prepare a well-mixed solution of m-CQD, 3 mL of m-CQD was added to 80 mL of ultra-pure water and mixed well for 10 min. Next, various amount of TC were added and mixed for 1 min, followed by investigating the fluorescence spectra of the resulting mixtures.

Regarding color tonality setup, smartphone-assisted visual detection was conducted using UV lamp with a 365 nm excitation wavelength at ambient temperature. In summary, 3 mL of m-CQD was added to 50 mL of distilled water and stirred continuously for 10 min. Following the addition of TC standard solutions, the mixtures were thoroughly stirred. Digital images of these solutions were then taken with a smartphone (iPhone 15 Pro, 2796 by 1290-pixel resolution at 460 ppi, Apple, USA) after 1 min. The RGB values of the images were taken and analyzed using either the Image J software, a pre-installed Android-based application.

The expired metformin tablet, the chemical structure of metformin is shown in Scheme 1, is mainly composed of 500 mg metformin and others carbonaceous excipients, such as sugar, sodium glutarate, and so on. Hydrothermal heating of these carbon-based compounds with nitrogen in metformin most likely produces N-doped graphitic carbon dots. Fig. 1 A-B displays the TEM image of the prepared N-doped carbon quantum dots (m-CQDs), illustrating sizes typically ranging from 2 to 4 nm based on the histogram shown in Fig. 1C.

The PXRD pattern (Fig. 1D) of m-CQDs shows a broad peak at 21 corresponding to 002 hkl plane (JCPDS card no. 26–1076). This suggests that the interlayer spacing (d) of the m-CQDs (0.42 nm) is greater than that of the graphitic interlayer spacing (0.33 nm), accompanied by broad characteristics. This observation confirms the non-crystalline nature of m-CQDs [33].

Fig. 1E displays the FTIR spectrum of the m-CQDs. The absorption peaks located at 3445, 3255, 2110, 1641, and 1351 cm−1 attributed to –OH, COOH, CC and C–O–C that are present in the m-CQDs [34]. The presence of these functional groups in the m-CQDs is responsible for their good dispersion in water.

The XPS survey spectrum of the m-CQDs as shown in Fig. 2 A shows three peaks at 285.08, 400.08, and 532.05 eV, which are attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s signals, respectively. The XPS of C 1s at 284.58, 286.17, and 287.18 eV (Fig. 2B) were attributed to the CC, CO, and O–CO bonds in the m-CQDs [35–39]. The XPS of O 1s at 531.95 and 531.90 eV (Fig. 2C) were attributed to the CO and O–CO bonds in the m-CQDs [35,40,41].

The N1speak can be deconvoluted into four components, centered at 399.24 eV, 400.11 eV, 400.39 eV, and 402.01 eV, which were assigned, respectively, to pyridinic, graphitic, pyrrolic, and pyridine N-oxide N-atoms [42–44].

Pure m-CQDs emit blue fluorescence peaked at 425 nm when excited at 350 nm as shown in the Fig. 2E, while this emission, undergoes shift to greenish-yellow at 530 nm upon introduction of TC. The tunning of emission is of highly interest from analytical perspective, as one can design ratiometric-based fluorometry and/or color tonality visual detection mode. Luckily, the emission gap, Δλemission, before and after the addition of TC is almost 105 nm. This Δλemission is broad enough to obtain various hues from the original emission color to the final emission color.

Several experimental studies were conducted to explore the sensing of TC by m-CQDs. The absorption spectra of m-CQDs, pure TC solution, and m-CQDs @TC were recorded and analyzed as shown in Fig. 3 A. The absorption spectrum of m-CQD @TC is evidently a combination of the absorption spectra of TC and m-CQD separately (Fig. 3A). This experiment rules out the possibility of a new complex forming between m-CQD and TC. Hence, the inner filter effect is most likely caused by the overlap between the absorption spectrum of the TC and the excitation spectrum of the emitter (m-CQD), leading to quenching specifically at 420 nm (Fig. 3A). Therefore, the presence of TC alongside m-CQD causes a reduction in the fluorescence emission of m-CQD (Fig. 3A). Thus, the intense greenish-yellow emission can be ascribed to aggregation-induced emission (AIE) resulting from TC binding to the m-CQD (Fig. 3B) [45]. It is worth mentioning that TEM images of the m-CQDs before and after adding TC were taken, and no any changes observed in the m-CQDs size and shape. When we mention aggregation, we are specifically referring to the aggregation or adsorbing of TC molecules on the carbon dot surface, not the aggregation of the m-CQDs themselves. In other words, the TC molecules cluster or adsorb on the surface of the carbon dots, but the carbon dots do not undergo aggregation.

In the ratiometric fluorometry analysis shown in Fig. 4A, a calibration curve was plotted by adding varying concentrations of TC at 30-second intervals under excitation at 350 nm. The fluorescence peak of m-CQDs at 425 nm decreased progressively, while a new peak at 530 nm appeared, causing the fluorescent color to shift from a vivid intense blue to a greenish-yellow.

On the other hand, and as a control experiment, without the presence of m-CQDs, the TC solution displayed a negligible broad fluorescence peak 510–530 nm, as depicted in Fig. 3B. This highlights the significant role of m-CQDs in dramatically enhancing the fluorescence of TC.

The ratio of greenish-yellow fluorescence intensity (F530) to the original blue emission (F420) of m-CQDs correlates directly with the amount of TC. The linear range detection of m-CQDs for TC was between 3 and 86 μM, and LOD recorded at 55 nM (at S/N = 3) (Fig. 4 B).
Additionally, for the sake of practical application, the fluorescence response of m-CQDs towards other potential interferences were studied, as depicted in Fig. 1SA and B.

Smartphone-based visual detection provides a convenient and accessible method for on-site testing, driven by its cost-effectiveness, simplicity, portability, and user-friendly interface [46–49]. These types of sensors facilitate rapid decision-making by delivering swift results without requiring sophisticated equipment, skilled personnel or specialized facilities, enhancing their utility in environments with limited resources. Here, a simple and convenient visual-based probe was further developed for the detection of TC. Fig. 4C displays blue emission in the solution under 365 nm excitation. Interestingly, due to the color altered from blue to green-yellow, as it is clear from Fig. 4C, the blue fluorescence of the CQDs gradually was changed to greenish-yellow with the amount of TC increasing from 6.0 to 78.0 µM (Fig. 4D). The LOD was 75 nM. This suggests that our ratiometric visual-based sensor is not just reliable, selective, sensitive, feasible, but also value-added, for on-site sensing of TC, which is very environmentally friendly. Table 1 shows a comparison between some materials used for detection of TC with our suggested probes.

To validate the effectiveness and practicality of the proposed ratiometric fluorescent and color tonality visual mode, tap water and pharmaceutical capsules were taken for TC detection. Tape water from Zakho city, Duhok, Iraq and pharmaceutical capsules were sourced from a local pharmacy in Zakho city, Duhok, Iraq. The samples were spiked with different amount of TC (Table 2 ). Recoveries and RSD (number of replicates = 3) illustrate that both proposed modes exhibit high accuracy and precision. The findings show that the devised ratiometric and visual modes are applicable for sensing TC in environmental and pharmaceutical areas.

Green Analytical Chemistry seeks to enhance the sustainability of analytical methods by reducing their environmental and human health risks. It promotes the use of fewer hazardous chemicals, lowers waste production, and conserves energy, all while ensuring reliable results. Greenness evaluation assesses how eco-friendly these methods are, using criteria like atom economy, E-factor, and tools such as the Green Analytical Procedure Index (GAPI). This approach supports safer and more efficient practices in scientific research. Several metrics have been developed to assess the greenness of analytical methods [55–60]. For the assessment of our method, three matrices were selected: ComplexMoGAPI and AGREE, while BAGI was used for assessing applicability.

ComplexMoGAPI is an innovative tool designed for the effective evaluation of analytical method greenness, merging visual appeal with precise scores for easier comparison of methods. Building on the original GAPI and its complementary tool ComplexGAPI, ComplexMoGAPI incorporates additional fields and a comprehensive scoring system to provide a detailed assessment of environmental sustainability. The tool evaluates factors such as yield, reagent use, energy consumption, and waste generation, assigning scores that reflect the overall environmental impact of each method. The software for ComplexMoGAPI simplifies the application process, enabling quicker and more straightforward evaluations. Available as open source, ComplexMoGAPI promotes transparency and wider adoption, offering a valuable resource for the chemical community to improve the sustainability of analytical procedures [55]. Analytical Greenness calculator (AGREE) is a comprehensive framework for the assessment of the degree of greenness of analytical methodologies based on the 12 principles of green analytical chemistry. It comprehensively incorporates each principle into assessment criteria that are transformed into numerical scores between 0 and 1. The approach offers flexibility by allowing user-assigned weights to distinguish parameter importance. The key outputs are an intuitive colored pictogram and report providing holistic and structured insight into the method's green profile. AGREE addresses limitations of previous metrics by delivering an informative yet accessible evaluation better aligned with green chemistry metrics [56]. Our method aligns with most greenness rules of both matrices. The only limitations are that the method produces more than 10 mL of waste without further treatment. Additionally, the method requires manual steps during sample preparation for measurement. These limitations are highlighted in ComplexMoGAPI Fig. 5 (A) as red, and reduced the AGREE score to 0.75 Fig. 5(B).

The Blue Applicability Grade Index (BAGI), a metric tool for evaluating the practicality of analytical methods in a complementary manner to established green chemistry assessment approaches. BAGI considers ten key criteria related to practical aspects such as analytical technique, sample throughput, required instrumentation, sample preparation needs, reagents, automation, and more. A sequential color scale is used to represent the final score from the assessment of these attributes, ranging from dark blue indicating high practical applicability to white representing low applicability. BAGI aims to facilitate the selection and development of optimally practical analytical procedures through a simple scoring system accessible via accompanying open-source software applications [61]. The BAGI evaluation emphasized the method's practicality, scoring 70.0. Main concerns regarding its applicability is that the method is limited to one analyte and requires the preparation of CDs within the laboratory Fig. 5(C).

Expired drugs, such as metformin, have been successfully converted into useful blue-emitting nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (m-CQDs) through an economical hydrothermal method. These blue-emitting m-CQDs shifted to a green-yellowish emission upon the addition of TC due with different in emission shift Δλemission = 105 nm. The disappearance of the blue emission is due to IFE while AIE is in charge of the greenish emission. Color tonality visual mode detection and a conventional ratiometric fluorescent method were developed. These methods were applied to pharmaceutical capsules and tap water samples. Our findings demonstrated excellent accuracy and precision in detecting TC, highlighting the potential of m-CQDs as a robust and effective tool in pharmaceutical and environmental monitoring applications. This innovative use of expired pharmaceuticals not only enhances drug safety by monitoring contaminants but also contributes to environmental sustainability by repurposing waste materials. This was confirmed by the high scores of the proposed method using ComplexMoGAPI, AGREE, and BAGI metrics. Additionally, the project added value by successfully converting pharmaceutical waste into low-cost, reliable sensor materials, showcasing an innovative way to address environmental sustainability and reduce waste.

Sameera Sh. Mohammed Ameen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Conceptualization. Alaa Bedair: Validation, Software, Investigation. Mahmoud Hamed: Validation, Software, Investigation. Fotouh R. Mansour: Writing – review & editing, Investigation. Khalid M. Omer: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111960.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
